diagrams.description: This timeline traces the intellectual history of political sociology. It begins with classical precursors (Aristotle's 4th century BCE classification of constitutions, Machiavelli's 1513 The Prince). The 18th–19th centuries brought Tocqueville's democracy and association (1835), and Marx & Engels' class politics (1848). The early 20th century saw Weber's 'Politics as a Vocation' (1919) and Gramsci's Prison Notebooks (1929‑35). Mid‑century, the field institutionalized with pluralism (Dahl, 1961) and elite theory. The 1960s‑70s added Lipset & Rokkan's cleavage theory (1967) and Skocpol's state-centered revolutions (1979). Late 20th century saw the rise of new social movements (Touraine, Habermas) and Putnam's social capital (1993). Contemporary work focuses on populism, digital politics, and global governance.
